title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
Note



This is a pre-course lecture. Watch the recorded video, then complete the Lecture 2 quiz on Canvas before the course starts.

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of this lecture you should be able to:

Explain what a program is
Navigate the MakeCode block editor
Describe the difference between on start and forever
Use variables, conditionals, loops, and functions in MakeCode
Explain how code gets from your browser onto the micro:bit

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
1. What is a program?
A program is a set of instructions, written in a language a machine can follow, that tells it exactly what to do — in what order, under what conditions, and how many times.
Consider a clinical protocol for managing a febrile patient:

Measure temperature
If temperature is above 38.5°C, administer paracetamol
Wait 30 minutes
Measure temperature again
If still above 38.5°C, escalate to the doctor
Repeat every hour

This is already a program. It has measurements, decisions, waiting, and repetition. Programming a microcontroller is the same kind of thinking — you are writing a protocol for a machine to follow.
The difference is that a machine follows instructions with complete literalness. It does exactly what you write, nothing more, nothing less. If your instructions are ambiguous or out of order, it will fail silently or behave unexpectedly. Precision matters.

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
2. MakeCode
MakeCode is a browser-based programming environment for the BBC micro:bit, developed by Microsoft. You do not need to install anything — it runs at makecode.microbit.org.
MakeCode uses blocks: coloured, puzzle-piece shaped instructions that you drag and snap together. This removes the need to type code manually, which reduces errors and lets you focus on logic rather than syntax.
The interface has three panels:

Left — simulator: a virtual micro:bit that runs your program in the browser, so you can test without hardware
Middle — toolbox: blocks organised by category (Basic, Input, Logic, Loops, Variables, Functions, and more)
Right — workspace: where you drag blocks and build your program


The simulator is useful but limited — it can only simulate the micro:bit’s built-in components. External sensors connected via the Sensorbit cannot be simulated; for those, you need the physical device.
You can switch between Blocks, JavaScript, and Python views using the buttons at the top of the screen. The same program looks different in each view, but they all do the same thing.

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
4. Variables
A variable is a named container for a value that can change.
Think of it as a labelled box. You put a number, a piece of text, or a true/false value into the box, give the box a name, and refer to it by that name later in your program.
set temperature to 0

forever:
    set temperature to [read temperature sensor]
    show temperature on display
Variables are essential because they let your program work with values it does not know in advance — like a sensor reading that changes every second.
Variable types you will use:

Number — any numeric value (37.2, 0, -5)
String — text (“Ready”, “Warning”, “Team A”)
Boolean — true or false only, used for on/off states and conditions







NoteJavaScript equivalent





let temperature = 0

basic.forever(function () {
    temperature = input.temperature()
    basic.showNumber(temperature)
})

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
6. Loops
A loop repeats a block of instructions.
forever is one kind of loop. But sometimes you want to repeat something a specific number of times, or only while a condition holds.
Repeat N times — runs exactly N times, then stops:
repeat 5 times:
    show heart icon
    wait 500 ms
    clear display
    wait 500 ms
While loop — repeats as long as a condition is true:
while temperature > 38.5:
    play alarm sound
    wait 1 second
    read temperature again
Loops require care. A loop with a condition that is never false will run forever and freeze your program. Always make sure a loop has a clear exit condition.






NoteJavaScript equivalent





// Repeat N times
for (let index = 0; index < 5; index++) {
    basic.showIcon(IconNames.Heart)
    basic.pause(500)
    basic.clearScreen()
    basic.pause(500)
}

// While loop
while (temperature > 38.5) {
    music.playTone(880, music.beat(BeatFraction.Whole))
    basic.pause(1000)
    temperature = input.temperature()
}

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
8. Reading a sensor
Reading a sensor means getting a value from a connected input device and storing it in a variable. The pattern is always the same:
set [variable name] to [sensor reading block]
For the micro:bit’s built-in temperature sensor this is straightforward. For external sensors connected via the Sensorbit, you will use extension blocks provided by Elecfreaks — we will look at those specifically in Lecture 3. The principle is identical.

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
section: 9. Getting your program onto the micro:bit
text:
9. Getting your program onto the micro:bit

Connect the micro:bit to your laptop with a USB cable
The micro:bit appears as a USB drive called MICROBIT
In MakeCode, click the Download button (bottom left of the workspace)
A .hex file is saved to your computer
Drag or copy the .hex file onto the MICROBIT drive
The orange LED on the micro:bit flashes while it writes the program
When it stops flashing, the program is running

You will do this many times during the lab. It takes about 10 seconds. You cannot permanently break a micro:bit by transferring a bad program — simply download and transfer again.

title: Lecture 2: Programming with MakeCode
text:
Summary

A program is a precise set of instructions for a machine to follow
MakeCode uses drag-and-drop blocks and runs in the browser at makecode.microbit.org
The interface has a simulator (left), toolbox (middle), and workspace (right)
on start runs once at startup; forever runs continuously
Variables store values that change, like sensor readings
Conditionals make decisions (if / else if / else)
Loops repeat instructions (repeat N times, while, forever)
Functions group reusable instructions under a name
Programs are transferred to the micro:bit as a .hex file via USB








TipReady for the quiz?



Complete the Lecture 2 quiz on Canvas before the course starts.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
Note



This is a pre-course lecture. Watch the recorded video, then complete the Lecture 1 quiz on Canvas before the course starts.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of this lecture you should be able to:

Explain what a microcontroller is and what it contains
Describe what the BBC micro:bit is and identify its main components
Distinguish between a sensor and an actuator
Distinguish between a digital and an analog signal
Give examples of sensors and actuators relevant to medicine and agriculture

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
1. What is a microcontroller?
Think about what a doctor does. They receive information — a patient’s temperature, pulse, breathing rate — they process it, make a decision, and then act: prescribing a drug, adjusting a drip, raising an alarm.
A microcontroller does exactly this, but with electricity instead of clinical judgment.
A microcontroller is a small, self-contained computer on a single chip. It has three essential parts:

A processor — the part that runs instructions, one step at a time
Memory — where the program and temporary data are stored
Input/output pins — physical connections to the outside world, where signals come in from sensors and go out to actuators

Microcontrollers are everywhere: in insulin pumps, pulse oximeters, irrigation timers, incubators, and refrigerators that keep vaccines cold. They are cheap, low-power, and reliable — which makes them ideal for low-resource settings.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
2. The BBC micro:bit
The micro:bit is a small microcontroller board designed for learning. It is about the size of a credit card. We will use it throughout this course.
It contains:

A processor and memory — the micro:bit V2 has a 64 MHz ARM processor and 512 KB of flash memory
A 5×5 LED matrix — 25 small lights that can display text, numbers, and simple images
Two buttons (A and B) — physical inputs you can press
An accelerometer — measures movement and tilt
A compass / magnetometer — detects magnetic fields and orientation
A temperature sensor — built into the processor chip
A microphone and speaker (V2 only)
Bluetooth and radio — for wireless communication between micro:bits or with a phone
Edge connector pins — 25 pins along the bottom edge for connecting external sensors and actuators

The edge connector is what makes the micro:bit extensible. By plugging in a breakout board — like the Elecfreaks Sensorbit we use in this course — you can connect many different sensors and actuators without soldering.
Explore the micro:bit interactively: scroll through the annotated diagram below to see each component up close.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
3. Signals: digital and analog
Before we talk about sensors and actuators, we need to understand two kinds of signals.
Digital signals have only two states: on or off, 1 or 0, high or low. A light switch is digital — it is either up or down, nothing in between. A button press is digital. A motion detector that says “motion detected / no motion” is digital.
Analog signals exist on a continuous scale. A dimmer switch is analog — you can set the light to any brightness between fully off and fully on. Temperature is analog — it can be 21.3°C, 21.35°C, or 37.2°C. Analog signals are converted to numbers by an analog-to-digital converter (ADC), which samples the signal and turns it into a value the processor can work with.
Why does this matter? Because sensors produce different kinds of signals, and you need to read them correctly in your program.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
4. Sensors
A sensor is a device that measures something in the physical world and converts it into an electrical signal.









Sensor
What it measures
Signal type
Example use




DHT11
Temperature and humidity
Digital (serial)
Monitor storage conditions for vaccines or grain


Soil moisture
Water content in soil
Analog
Trigger irrigation when soil is too dry


PIR
Movement of warm bodies
Digital
Detect a patient entering a room; scare animals from crops


MQ3 alcohol
Alcohol vapor in air
Analog
Screen for alcohol in breath; monitor fermentation


UV
Ultraviolet light intensity
Analog
Measure sun exposure; optimise plant growth


Crash sensor
Physical contact / pressure
Digital
Detect if a door or container has been opened



Some important things to understand about sensors:

Every sensor has a range. The DHT11 measures temperature between 0°C and 50°C. Outside that range, the reading is wrong or absent. Choosing the right sensor for your use case means checking the range.
Every sensor has accuracy limits. The DHT11 is accurate to ±2°C. For monitoring a patient’s fever that may be acceptable; for calibrating a laboratory instrument it is not.
Sensors need time. The MQ3 alcohol sensor requires a warm-up period before its readings are reliable. The DHT11 can only be read once every two seconds.
Sensors can fail or drift. A soil moisture sensor left in the ground for months may corrode and give false readings.

Evaluating a sensor means asking: does it measure the right thing, at the right range, with enough accuracy, reliably enough, for my use case?

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
5. Actuators
An actuator is a device that converts an electrical signal into a physical action.








Actuator
What it does
Example use




LED / Rainbow LED
Emits light in different colours
Status indicator; phototherapy prototype


OLED screen
Displays text and graphics
Show readings to a user


Servo motor (180°)
Rotates to a specific angle
Open a valve; move a flap


DC motor with fan
Spins continuously
Ventilation; cooling



Actuators are the outputs of your system. A well-designed device combines sensors with actuators: a temperature sensor detects that a storage room is too hot, and a fan actuator turns on to cool it down.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
section: 6. Putting it together: sense → decide → act
text:
6. Putting it together: sense → decide → act
Every microcontroller-based system follows the same basic pattern:

A sensor measures something and sends a signal to the microcontroller
The processor runs your program, which reads the signal and decides what to do
The program sends a signal to an actuator, which does something in the world

Example: A simple hand sanitiser dispenser for a clinic entrance.

A PIR sensor detects that someone has approached
The micro:bit detects the signal and checks: “is there motion?”
If yes, it sends a signal to a servo motor that presses the sanitiser pump

This is not a complicated program. It is a real product. This is what we will learn to build.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
section: 7. Why this matters in medicine and agriculture
text:
7. Why this matters in medicine and agriculture
Microcontrollers are particularly valuable in settings where:

Continuous monitoring is needed but trained staff are limited
Automation can reduce human error
Cost is a constraint
Power supply may be intermittent

In Uganda and across sub-Saharan Africa, there are real problems these tools can address:

Cold chain monitoring for vaccines and blood products
Automated irrigation for small-scale farms
Environmental monitoring in patient wards
Early warning systems for crop disease or pest activity
Point-of-care diagnostic aids

This course will not solve all of these problems. But by the end of it, you will have the practical knowledge to begin.

title: Lecture 1: Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Actuators
text:
Summary

A microcontroller has a processor, memory, and input/output pins
The BBC micro:bit is a microcontroller with many built-in sensors and a connector for external ones
Digital signals have two states; analog signals are continuous
Sensors convert physical phenomena into electrical signals
Actuators convert electrical signals into physical action
Every system follows the pattern: sense → decide → act








TipReady for the quiz?



Complete the Lecture 1 quiz on Canvas before the course starts on 16 June.

title: Microcontrollers for Health & Agriculture
text:
The BBC micro:bit V2 — front
Scroll down to explore the main components.




This is the complete front of the board — roughly the size of a credit card. The numbered labels identify each feature. We will work through them one by one.




The 5×5 LED matrix is the most visible feature on the front. 25 individually controllable red LEDs can display scrolling text, numbers, arrows, and custom images. The matrix also works as a light sensor — it can detect ambient brightness without any extra components.




Button A is on the left side of the board. In MakeCode, pressing it triggers an on button A pressed event. You can programme any action in response — displaying a reading, toggling a light, or sending a radio message.




Button B mirrors Button A on the right. You can programme A and B independently, or detect when both are pressed together for a third action.




The gold touch logo at the top is a capacitive touch sensor — unique to the V2. Your finger doesn’t need to press it: just touching it is enough.




The small dot to the right of the logo is the microphone activity LED. It lights up whenever the built-in microphone is recording — a privacy indicator so you always know when sound is being measured.




The gold band along the bottom is the edge connector. The three large holes — labelled 0, 1, and 2 — are general-purpose input/output pins: connect sensors here with crocodile clips or the Sensorbit. 3V supplies power to connected components; GND completes the circuit.




That covers the front. Now let’s flip it over.




The BBC micro:bit V2 — back
Most of the processing and sensing happens here.




Here is the full back of the board. The large chips, the antenna trace, and the connectors are all visible. We will go through each in turn.




The gold zig-zag trace in the top left is the Bluetooth and radio antenna — printed directly onto the board, no separate component needed. It handles both Bluetooth Low Energy (for phone connectivity) and the micro:bit’s own 2.4 GHz radio protocol (for micro:bit-to-micro:bit communication).




The micro USB port at the top centre is how you connect the micro:bit to your laptop to download programmes. It also powers the board from USB. When you drag a .hex file onto the MICROBIT drive, it enters through this port.




The square component in the centre is the speaker. It can play tones, melodies, and sound effects directly, with no extra wiring. In MakeCode, use the Music category.




The small component close to the speaker is the backside of the microphone — on the front you will see it as a little whole. It detects sound level and can recognise loud events such as claps or shouts. The LED you saw on the front indicates when it is active.




The battery connector on the right edge accepts a standard 2× AAA battery pack. This is how you power the micro:bit away from a laptop — essential for portable projects and demos.




The chip left of the speaker is the processor — a Nordic nRF52833 running at 64 MHz with 512 KB of flash memory. Your programme lives here. It also contains a built-in temperature sensor that measures the chip temperature — a rough proxy for air temperature.




The two chips on the bottom left are the accelerometer and compass. The accelerometer measures forces in three dimensions — tilt, shake, freefall, movement. The compass detects magnetic north. Both are available as built-in sensor blocks in MakeCode, no extra wiring needed.




That is the complete micro:bit V2. Front and back, now you know what every component does.

title: Interactive: Explore the micro:bit
text: Scroll through the diagram below to explore every component on the BBC micro:bit V2 — front and back. Each step zooms in on a different part and explains what it does.

title: Microcontroller Programming and Interfacing for Medical and Agricultural Technology
text:
Welcome to this intensive two-day course on microcontroller programming and interfacing. The course is designed for students with a medical or life-science background — no prior programming experience is assumed.
We use the BBC micro:bit and the Elecfreaks Smart Health Kit as our hardware platform, and MakeCode as our programming environment.

title: Microcontroller Programming and Interfacing for Medical and Agricultural Technology
text:
Course goals
By the end of this course you will be able to:

Understand the process of ideation and innovation. Evaluate innovation cultures. Apply your knowledge by participating in brainstorming and team-building activities.
Understand basic microcontroller architecture.
Understand program flow, variables, loops, procedures, and querying peripherals with MakeCode.
Understand common sensors and actuators. Evaluate and choose alternatives in the context of use cases.
Understand the sensors and actuators provided with the Elecfreaks Smart Health Kit. Evaluate their utility and limitations.
Apply your knowledge by connecting or activating peripherals. Write MakeCode under supervision. Evaluate your results and propose ways to improve.
Create a prototype of a simple but potentially useful product. Evaluate the results and propose future improvements.

title: Microcontroller Programming and Interfacing for Medical and Agricultural Technology
text:
How this course works
This course follows a flipped, spaced-repetition format:








Exposure
When
What




First
Before the course
Watch the lecture videos and answer the Canvas quizzes


Second
In-class discussion
Revisit and deepen the same content together


Third
Lab and project
Apply the knowledge with real hardware



This means the pre-course lectures and quizzes are not optional — they are the foundation for everything that happens in the room.

title: Microcontroller Programming and Interfacing for Medical and Agricultural Technology
text:
Teams
You will work in five teams of four to five students. Teams are formed during the mixer on the evening of 16 June.
Each team will have access to one BBC micro:bit and one Elecfreaks Smart Health Kit, as well as share other kits with everyone for the group project on day 2.
